overall_summary: The community's top notebooks primarily focus on robust baseline pipelines and extensive exploratory data analysis for the MABe mouse behavior detection challenge. A strong emphasis is placed on FPS-aware temporal feature engineering, memory-efficient data processing, and training per-action binary classifiers using tree-based models like LightGBM, XGBoost, and CatBoost. Advanced techniques include Optuna-optimized ensemble weighting, adaptive probability thresholding, and rigorous post-processing to convert noisy frame-level predictions into clean action segments.
competition_flows: Loads CSV metadata and parquet tracking data, engineers FPS-scaled temporal and pairwise features, trains per-action binary XGBoost models with StratifiedGroupKFold, optimizes thresholds via Optuna, converts frame-wise probabilities to action segments, outputs cleaned submission CSV.

Loads CSV metadata and parquet tracking data, generates FPS-scaled kinematic and interaction features, trains ensemble of LightGBM/XGBoost/CatBoost per behavior class, applies adaptive temporal thresholding, outputs frame-level behavior detection submission.

Reads training and test CSVs alongside parquet tracking/annotation files, generates batched distance-based features, trains per-action binary LightGBM classifiers using GroupKFold, outputs rule-compliant submission CSV.

Loads CSV metadata and parquet tracking/annotation files, extracts FPS-scaled time-series features, loads pre-trained CatBoost/XGBoost models, optimizes ensemble weights and thresholds via Optuna, generates robustified submission CSV.

Loads CSV metadata and parquet tracking data, generates FPS-aware temporal and interaction features, trains weighted ensemble of LightGBM/XGBoost/CatBoost per action, applies adaptive temporal thresholding, robustifies output into final submission.

Loads CSV metadata and parquet tracking files, extracts FPS-scaled spatial-temporal features, trains ensemble of tree models per action, applies adaptive temporal thresholding, outputs frame-level action localization submission.

Loads test tracking data, generates custom self/pair features, loads pre-trained XGBoost models and thresholds from multiple folds, applies thresholded predictions, merges consecutive frames into events, formats final submission CSV.

Loads raw tracking and behavioral data, extracts spatial-temporal features, trains ensemble of tree-based classifiers with stratified sampling, combines predictions, generates submission alongside diagnostic visualizations.
Loads training CSV and corresponding Parquet tracking/annotation files, renders and displays skeletal frames from multiple mouse videos to inspect data quality and annotation alignment.

Loads CSV metadata and parquet tracking files, groups videos by body-part configuration, extracts spatial/temporal features, trains per-action LightGBM classifiers, generates frame-level predictions with thresholding and post-processing, outputs CSV submission.

Parsing behaviors_labeled strings into agent/target/behavior columns
Loading metadata and tracking data with pandas/polars
Normalizing pixel coordinates using pix_per_cm_approx during reading

Normalizes coordinates to centimeters using pix_per_cm_approx
Handles missing values with SimpleImputer or fallback values
Merges overlapping predictions and fills temporal gaps
Filters out MABe22 lab videos or clips with 'lights on' condition
Converts coordinates/features to numpy float32 arrays
Subsamples training data to manage memory constraints
Processes data in memory-efficient generators or batches
Splits data into single-mouse and pair-mouse subsets
Applies rolling mean smoothing to prediction probabilities
Filters events shorter than 3 frames as noise
Computes derived metrics (video duration, total frames, arena area, pixel density)
Applies stratified sampling for class balance
Filters warnings and handles missing annotations gracefully
features_engineering: FPS-scaled rolling windows for speed, acceleration, curvature, turn rate, and state transitions
Exponential weighted means and speed state binning with transition counts
Relative distance, lead angles, chase/approach metrics, and velocity alignment/correlation for mouse pairs
Pairwise body part distances, elongation ratios, and orientation angles
Ear distance shifts and nose-tail distance lags
Euclidean distances between all tracked body parts for translation/rotation invariance
Speed features derived from frame-shifted coordinates (e.g., 10-frame lag)
Multiscale speed features (mean, std, ratio) and long-range rolling statistics
Symmetric KL divergence of Gaussian fits on speed distributions
Custom self and pair behavioral features via modular processing scripts
Spatial history, displacement/activity metrics, position coordinates, and temporal aggregations
Squared Euclidean distances and cosine of body orientation angles
ensembling: Frame-wise combination across independent binary classifiers per action
Optuna-optimized weight tuning on out-of-fold predictions
Weighted averaging of per-action binary classifiers
Fold-specific threshold combination and max-score selection
Combination of tree-based models trained on different feature sections
insights: FPS-aware scaling is critical for temporal features across videos with different frame rates.
Stratifying by video_id prevents data leakage during cross-validation.
Training binary classifiers per action handles class imbalance better than multiclass approaches.
Optimizing per-action thresholds via Optuna improves F1 over fixed thresholds.
Temporal windows and lags must be scaled by the video's frames per second to remain duration-invariant across videos with different sampling rates.
Class imbalance is handled dynamically via scale_pos_weight and auto_class_weights rather than static resampling or threshold shifting.
Adaptive per-action probability thresholds combined with temporal smoothing significantly reduce false positives in frame-level behavior detection.
Distance-based features provide necessary translation and rotation invariance for recognizing animal behaviors across varying positions.
Missing multi-class labels can be effectively handled by training independent binary classifiers per action and applying a probability threshold.
Highly redundant 30 FPS tracking data can be safely subsampled without losing predictive signal, enabling out-of-memory processing.
Submission robustness requires strict deduplication of overlapping frame predictions and explicit coverage of all test videos.
Raw 2D pose coordinates can be transformed into rich motion features by scaling temporal windows to physical time and computing derivatives, rolling statistics, and relative kinematics.
Per-action gradient boosting models can be effectively combined using Optuna-optimized weights and thresholds rather than fixed averaging or equal weighting.
Careful post-processing (handling sparse labels, overlapping frames, and empty videos) is critical for valid submission formats in frame-level action recognition.
Per-action binary classification with a weighted ensemble outperforms single multi-class models for this event detection task.
Adaptive thresholding combined with temporal smoothing effectively converts noisy frame-wise probabilities into clean event boundaries.
Temporal windows and lags must be scaled by the video's FPS to maintain consistent physical durations across videos with different frame rates.
Stratified subsampling helps manage memory and class imbalance during tree model training.
Adaptive per-action thresholds combined with rolling smoothing significantly improve frame-level localization precision.
Custom frame-overlap scoring is necessary because standard metrics do not account for variable-length action spans.
Uses Polars for efficient data manipulation and feature generation.
Demonstrates a modular approach by separating feature generation, model inference, and post-processing into distinct steps.
Shows how to handle multi-class behavior prediction by selecting the maximum probability per frame.
Illustrates event-based post-processing to convert frame-level predictions into continuous action segments.
Comprehensive distributional and correlation analysis of features is essential for debugging and understanding model inputs.
Stratified sampling effectively maintains class balance during training.
Comparing prediction distributions against ground truth reveals alignment gaps and guides feature refinement.
The visualization pipeline clarifies how skeletal keypoints are structured across frames and mice, making it easier to verify tracking completeness and annotation boundaries.
Pivoting Parquet tracking data on mouse_id and bodypart efficiently aligns multi-keypoint coordinates per frame for direct plotting.
Inspecting raw pose data before modeling is critical to identify missing parts, coordinate noise, or misaligned action annotations.
Processing data separately by body_parts_tracked configuration is necessary due to varying feature dimensions.
Single-mouse and pair-mouse tracking data provide complementary signals for behavior recognition.
Temporal features like speed and orientation significantly enhance spatial coordinate representations.
Memory constraints require strategic downsampling and dropping less informative body parts.
critical_findings: The custom evaluation metric requires careful handling of sparse labels and overlapping predictions.
Videos with no predictions must be filled with dummy segments to avoid submission errors.
Dropping specific headpiece/tail tracking points reduces noise without losing behavioral signal.
MABe22 clips with 'lights on' condition are likely sleeping and introduce label noise, so they should be dropped.
Tracking data contains noisy headpiece and tail points that should be filtered out to prevent feature corruption.
The competition metric requires frame-level overlap calculation, making temporal consistency and post-processing crucial for high scores.
Videos tracked with more than five body parts contain noisy headpiece and tail components that degrade performance and should be filtered.
Certain lab datasets (e.g., MABe22) lack annotation files entirely and must be excluded during training to prevent crashes.
The competition scoring metric requires careful handling of sparse labels, as predictions for unlabelled agent-target pairs must be ignored.
The model explicitly underperforms on 'single' targets, prompting the author to train on more samples to improve coverage.
Specific headpiece and tail tracking parts are identified as noisy or redundant and are systematically dropped during preprocessing.
The model explicitly underperforms on 'single' targets, prompting the author to increase training samples for that subset.
Multiple predictions for the same frame from one agent/target pair are treated as errors, and events shorter than 3 frames are filtered as likely noise.
Some tracking videos contain missing coordinate values that must be handled during pivoting.
The MABe22_ laboratory videos are excluded from processing.
Memory limits necessitate dropping specific headpiece and tail body parts and downsampling training data to 100k samples.
what_did_not_work: The model underperforms on 'single' targets, so the author increased the training sample size for that subset instead of modifying the architecture or features.
notable_individual_insights: votes 656 (Social Action Recognition in Mice | XGBoost): FPS-aware scaling and StratifiedGroupKFold by video_id are critical to prevent data leakage and ensure temporal consistency.
votes 572 (MABe extra trees GPU): Dynamic class imbalance handling via scale_pos_weight and auto_class_weights outperforms static resampling for severe label skew.
votes 381 (MABe Nearest Neighbors): Distance-based features provide essential translation and rotation invariance, while subsampling 30 FPS data enables out-of-memory processing.
votes 357 (Social Action Recognition in Mice | Ensemble): Optuna-optimized ensemble weights per action class significantly outperform fixed averaging or equal weighting.
votes 257 (MABe | LGB + XGB + CatBoost): Adaptive temporal thresholding combined with gap-filling robustly converts noisy frame-wise probabilities into clean submission events.
votes 162 (MABe EDA which makes sense): A custom skeletal visualization pipeline is indispensable for verifying tracking completeness and annotation alignment before modeling.
votes 156 (MABe Challenge - EDA + Starter Model): Processing data separately by body_parts_tracked configuration is necessary due to varying feature dimensions across videos.
